past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 5 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 3 years ago, managed with metformin
Dental abscess of lower left molar, treated with extraction and oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 3 weeks ago
History of recurrent dental infections over the past year
allergies: Penicillin (childhood rash, tolerates amoxicillin without issue per allergist evaluation)
family_history: Father: died of colon cancer at age 67
Mother: alive, age 74, hypertension and osteoarthritis
Brother: alive, age 52, multiple sclerosis diagnosed at age 38
No family history of brain tumors or seizure disorders

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 10 pack-year history, quit 8 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two teenage children in suburban home
social_history.recent_stressors: Ongoing dental problems requiring multiple visits; concerned about missing work

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Cooperative but mildly anxious. Speech fluent without paraphasic errors. Language comprehension intact for simple and complex commands. Naming intact to confrontation. Able to register 3 objects but recalls only 2 of 3 at 5 minutes. Attention mildly impaired — makes one error on serial 7s. Follows three-step commands without difficulty. No neglect on line bisection testing.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3.5 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally — no field cut identified on bedside testing. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Facial sensation intact bilaterally to light touch and pinprick. Facial symmetry preserved. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength normal. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone normal in all extremities. Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities, including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, hip flexors, quadriceps, hamstrings, ankle dorsiflexors, and plantar flexors. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride, base, and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: The neurological examination is largely non-focal, which is atypical for a large structural lesion and complicates the differential. The mild memory impairment and attentional deficit could reflect postictal state, subclinical seizure activity, or early mass effect. The lack of focal motor or sensory deficits despite imaging findings is noteworthy. No signs of meningismus. No Kernig or Brudzinski signs.

patient.history_present_illness: This 48-year-old right-handed man with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a recent dental abscess was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his wife witnessed a generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. The patient was watching television at approximately 8 PM when his wife noticed him stare blankly for several seconds before his eyes rolled back and he developed bilateral arm and leg stiffening followed by rhythmic jerking. The episode lasted approximately two minutes, after which he was confused and drowsy for about twenty minutes. He has no prior history of seizures. On further questioning, his wife reports that over the past two to three weeks he has seemed 'not himself' — he has been unusually irritable, has complained of intermittent mild headaches primarily in the morning that improve as the day progresses, and has had difficulty concentrating on grading papers. She initially attributed these changes to stress from his ongoing dental problems. Three weeks ago, he underwent extraction of an infected lower left molar that had been causing him significant pain for several weeks. He completed a full course of amoxicillin-clavulanate and his dentist confirmed the extraction site was healing well at a follow-up visit one week ago. However, his wife now recalls that approximately two weeks ago he had a single night where he woke drenched in sweat with a mild temperature, which resolved with ibuprofen and did not recur. In the emergency department, a CT scan of the head was performed which revealed a ring-enhancing lesion in the left frontal lobe with surrounding edema. Given his recent dental infection with possible transient bacteremia, the emergency physician raised concern for a brain abscess and started empiric intravenous antibiotics with ceftriaxone and metronidazole. However, the admitting neurology team noted that the ring enhancement pattern on CT was somewhat irregular and thick-walled, and requested an urgent MRI brain with and without contrast for further characterization. The patient was admitted to the neurology service for further workup. He currently denies headache, nausea, or visual changes, and feels 'back to normal' apart from being tired.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening

findings.morphology: Intermittent bursts of semi-rhythmic theta activity superimposed on an otherwise well-organized background
findings.clinical_correlation: Non-specific diffuse cerebral dysfunction; differential includes postictal state, metabolic disturbance, medication effect, or diffuse cerebral process

findings.morphology: Intermittent polymorphic delta activity, moderate amplitude, occurring in brief runs of 3-8 seconds
findings.clinical_correlation: Focal slowing in left frontotemporal region raises concern for underlying structural pathology; however, can also be seen as a postictal phenomenon

artifacts.description: Intermittent bilateral temporal muscle artifact, not significantly obscuring underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to recent seizure and concern for intracranial pathology
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating intermittent generalized theta slowing and focal left frontotemporal polymorphic delta activity. No epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures captured during this 40-minute recording. The focal slowing in the left frontotemporal region correlates with the known left frontal lesion on neuroimaging and could reflect any focal structural pathology including neoplasm, abscess, or demyelinating lesion. Absence of epileptiform discharges does not exclude an epileptic tendency given the clinical history of a witnessed seizure. Clinical correlation with neuroimaging and further workup recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording; continuous EEG monitoring may be warranted to assess for subclinical seizures or further characterize epileptiform activity.
recommended_actions: Consider continuous EEG monitoring if additional seizures occur
Anti-seizure medication management given clinical seizure and structural lesion

findings.location: Left frontal lobe, centered in the subcortical white matter extending to the cortical surface, involving the posterior aspect of the middle frontal gyrus

signal_characteristics.T1: Central hypointensity with isointense to slightly hyperintense rim
signal_characteristics.T2: Central hyperintensity (likely necrosis or liquefaction) with heterogeneous iso-to-hyperintense rim
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive surrounding hyperintensity extending through the left frontal white matter into the corona radiata, crossing into the genu of the corpus callosum
signal_characteristics.DWI: Peripheral rim shows mildly restricted diffusion; central region shows restricted diffusion that could represent either high cellularity or viscous content (equivocal)
signal_characteristics.ADC: ADC values in the central cavity are intermediate — lower than expected for simple cystic necrosis but not as markedly low as typical purulent material
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Thick, irregular ring enhancement with nodular components along the medial wall; enhancement thickness varies from 3 mm to 12 mm

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the left lateral ventricle frontal horn with 3 mm rightward midline shift at the level of the septum pellucidum
findings.borders: Irregularly marginated with finger-like projections of FLAIR signal extending along white matter tracts

findings.location: Left frontal subcortical white matter, 1.5 cm anterior to the dominant lesion

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, contiguous with surrounding edema from dominant lesion

additional_observations: Extensive vasogenic edema throughout the left frontal white matter
FLAIR signal extends across the genu of the corpus callosum into the right frontal white matter — could represent edema tracking or infiltrative tumor crossing the midline
No other enhancing lesions in the brain
No acute hemorrhage, though faint susceptibility artifact along the medial rim may suggest prior microhemorrhage or calcification
Mucosal thickening in the left maxillary sinus, likely related to recent dental procedure
mri.impression: Left frontal ring-enhancing lesion with irregular, thick-walled enhancement, surrounding vasogenic edema, mild mass effect, and a satellite enhancing nodule. The differential diagnosis includes high-grade glioma (glioblastoma), brain abscess, and less likely tumefactive demyelination or CNS lymphoma. The thick, irregular enhancement with nodular components and corpus callosum involvement favor a neoplastic process, while the recent dental infection with possible bacteremia and equivocal central diffusion restriction raise the possibility of brain abscess. The satellite lesion could represent either multifocal infection or infiltrative tumor. Advanced imaging with MR spectroscopy and perfusion imaging is recommended for further characterization.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Thick, irregular ring enhancement with nodular components, corpus callosum involvement, satellite enhancing lesion, and white matter tract infiltration all favor high-grade glioma. However, the central restricted diffusion is equivocal.

Recent dental abscess with possible transient bacteremia provides a plausible source. Ring enhancement and central restricted diffusion can be seen in abscesses. However, the wall is thicker and more irregular than typical abscess, and corpus callosum crossing is uncommon in abscess.

Patient has a brother with MS. Ring enhancement could represent tumefactive MS. However, the enhancement pattern is not typical open-ring, there is significant mass effect, and the age/presentation is less typical.

Can cross the corpus callosum and enhance strongly. However, lymphoma typically shows homogeneous rather than ring enhancement in immunocompetent patients, and restricted diffusion is usually more uniform.

recommended_actions: MR perfusion imaging to evaluate for elevated rCBV (favors high-grade neoplasm over abscess)
MR spectroscopy to evaluate choline/NAA ratio and presence of amino acid peaks (abscess) vs lipid/lactate (tumor)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if neoplasm suspected to evaluate for primary malignancy
Consider stereotactic biopsy for definitive diagnosis
Blood cultures if abscess remains in differential

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; could be stress response (postictal), steroid effect, infection, or non-specific. Does not reliably distinguish abscess from tumor.
Mild neutrophilia; non-specific, may be postictal, stress-related, or reflect underlying infection

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated; consistent with known type 2 diabetes, stress hyperglycemia, or both. Does not distinguish between differential diagnoses.

Inflammatory markers.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; non-specific. Could reflect recent dental infection (now treated), occult infection, systemic inflammatory response to tumor, or postictal inflammation. Does not reliably distinguish abscess from high-grade glioma.
Mildly elevated; non-specific inflammatory marker. Consistent with infection, malignancy, or other inflammatory process.
Normal; argues somewhat against active systemic bacterial infection, but does not exclude a walled-off intracranial abscess

Other.clinical_significance: Within normal range; LDH can be mildly elevated in high-grade malignancies but is non-specific
Elevated; consistent with suboptimally controlled type 2 diabetes. Relevant for surgical planning and wound healing.
Negative blood cultures; reduces but does not eliminate probability of brain abscess, especially if antibiotic pretreated

labs.interpretation: Laboratory workup shows mild leukocytosis with neutrophilia, mildly elevated inflammatory markers (CRP 1.8, ESR 28), hyperglycemia consistent with known diabetes, and an elevated HbA1c. Coagulation studies, hepatic panel, and renal function are normal, providing a suitable pre-surgical baseline. The inflammatory marker elevation is a key diagnostic confounder: it could reflect the recent dental infection (now treated), residual inflammation, an active intracranial abscess, or a systemic inflammatory response to high-grade malignancy. Normal procalcitonin argues somewhat against active systemic bacterial infection. Negative blood cultures at 48 hours reduce the likelihood of ongoing bacteremia but do not exclude a walled-off intracranial abscess, especially given recent antibiotic therapy.

findings.type: Elevated cerebral blood volume in enhancing rim
findings.location: Left frontal ring-enhancing lesion, enhancing rim

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Maximum rCBV ratio 3.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter (markedly elevated)
signal_characteristics.rCBV_central: rCBV in central non-enhancing region is low (0.4), consistent with necrosis
signal_characteristics.perfusion_pattern: Heterogeneous elevated perfusion in the enhancing rim, particularly along the lateral and posterior walls

additional_observations: rCBV ratio >1.75 in the enhancing rim is highly suggestive of high-grade neoplasm rather than abscess or demyelination
Brain abscesses typically show low rCBV in the enhancing wall (rCBV <1.0)
Tumefactive demyelination typically shows low to intermediate rCBV
The markedly elevated rCBV of 3.8 is most consistent with high-grade glioma (WHO grade 4)
output.impression: MR perfusion imaging demonstrates markedly elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV ratio 3.8) in the enhancing rim of the left frontal lesion, with low rCBV centrally. This perfusion pattern is highly suggestive of a high-grade neoplasm, most likely glioblastoma. The elevated rCBV strongly argues against brain abscess (which typically shows low rCBV in the enhancing wall) and tumefactive demyelination. The findings support prioritizing stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection over prolonged empiric antibiotic therapy.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: rCBV ratio >3.0 in enhancing rim is highly characteristic of high-grade glioma with neovascularity
Abscess walls typically show low rCBV (<1.0) due to inflammatory vasculature without neovascularity

recommended_actions: Proceed with MR spectroscopy for additional characterization
Strongly consider early stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection

findings.location: Left frontal lesion, enhancing rim (multivoxel technique)

signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks present
signal_characteristics.amino_acids: No definite amino acid peaks (acetate, succinate, alanine) identified

findings.location: Left frontal lesion, central non-enhancing region

signal_characteristics.dominant_peaks: Lipid and lactate dominate the spectrum

additional_observations: Elevated choline with reduced NAA in the enhancing rim is characteristic of high-grade neoplasm with high cellular turnover
Prominent lipid/lactate peaks reflect necrosis and anaerobic metabolism, typical of glioblastoma
Absence of amino acid peaks (acetate, succinate) argues against brain abscess, where bacterial metabolites typically produce characteristic amino acid peaks
The spectroscopic pattern is most consistent with WHO grade 4 glioma (glioblastoma)
output.impression: MR spectroscopy demonstrates markedly elevated choline, reduced NAA, and prominent lipid/lactate peaks in the enhancing rim, with a necrotic central cavity. No amino acid peaks suggestive of abscess are identified. These findings are characteristic of a high-grade glioma (glioblastoma) and argue strongly against brain abscess. Combined with the perfusion findings showing elevated rCBV, the imaging profile is highly consistent with glioblastoma.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Classic spectroscopic pattern with elevated Cho/NAA, Cho/Cr ratios and lipid/lactate peaks without amino acid peaks
Absence of amino acid peaks (acetate, succinate, alanine) essentially excludes pyogenic abscess

recommended_actions: Proceed with stereotactic biopsy or craniotomy for tissue diagnosis
Molecular profiling including IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q codeletion

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis Report.value: Clear, no pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural effusion.
Normal size and attenuation. No focal lesions.
Normal bilaterally. No masses or hydronephrosis.
No suspicious masses. Normal wall thickness.
CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis Report.clinical_significance: Relevant given family history of colon cancer

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis Report.value: No lytic or blastic lesions. Degenerative changes in lumbar spine.
Mucosal thickening in left maxillary sinus, consistent with post-dental extraction changes.
CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis Report.clinical_significance: Residual sinus inflammation from recent dental procedure; provides context for dental abscess history but no evidence of ongoing active infection

output.interpretation: CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast is unremarkable. No evidence of a primary malignancy, metastatic disease, or lymphadenopathy. The absence of an extracranial primary tumor supports a primary brain neoplasm rather than metastatic disease. Incidental mucosal thickening in the left maxillary sinus is consistent with the recent dental procedure. This normal body CT, combined with the imaging characteristics of the brain lesion, supports glioblastoma as the leading diagnosis.
abnormal_values_summary: Left maxillary sinus mucosal thickening (post-dental extraction, clinically insignificant)

Neuropathology.value: High-grade glial neoplasm with marked hypercellularity, nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis. Consistent with glioblastoma, WHO grade 4.
Neuropathology.clinical_significance: Definitive tissue diagnosis of glioblastoma, WHO grade 4
IDH-wildtype status; consistent with primary (de novo) glioblastoma, associated with poorer prognosis compared to IDH-mutant gliomas

Neuropathology.value: No IDH1 or IDH2 mutations detected

Neuropathology.clinical_significance: Unmethylated MGMT promoter predicts reduced benefit from temozolomide chemotherapy; associated with worse overall prognosis
Absence of 1p/19q codeletion excludes oligodendroglioma; consistent with astrocytic lineage
Retained ATRX expression is typical of IDH-wildtype glioblastoma
Partial p53 accumulation; non-specific finding in glioblastoma
High proliferation index consistent with aggressive WHO grade 4 neoplasm
EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma and is one of the molecular criteria for glioblastoma diagnosis per WHO 2021 classification
TERT promoter mutation, along with EGFR amplification, meets molecular criteria for glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype per WHO 2021 CNS5 classification

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy confirms the diagnosis of glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4, per WHO 2021 CNS tumor classification. The tumor demonstrates classic histological features including microvascular proliferation and palisading necrosis, as well as molecular markers characteristic of primary glioblastoma (EGFR amplification, TERT promoter mutation, no IDH mutations). The MGMT promoter is unmethylated, which predicts reduced benefit from temozolomide chemotherapy. This information is critical for treatment planning and prognostic counseling. Standard-of-care treatment involves maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent radiation therapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) and temozolomide, followed by adjuvant temozolomide, although the benefit of temozolomide is reduced in the setting of unmethylated MGMT.

signal_characteristics.left_hand_motor: Activation in right precentral gyrus, normal location, >2 cm from tumor margin
signal_characteristics.right_hand_motor: Activation in left precentral gyrus, approximately 1.2 cm from the posterior-lateral margin of the enhancing tumor
signal_characteristics.bilateral_foot_motor: Activation at vertex, >3 cm from tumor

signal_characteristics.brocas_area: Strong activation in left inferior frontal gyrus (pars opercularis and triangularis), located 0.8 cm from the inferior margin of the tumor. Some activation appears to be displaced anteriorly and inferiorly by the mass.
signal_characteristics.wernickes_area: Activation in left posterior superior temporal gyrus, distant from tumor
signal_characteristics.supplementary_motor_area: Activation noted in left SMA, 1.5 cm from superior tumor margin

additional_observations: The tumor is in close proximity to the left Broca area (0.8 cm), placing expressive language function at risk during surgical resection
Right hand motor cortex is approximately 1.2 cm from the tumor, placing fine motor control at moderate risk
Language lateralization is strongly left-hemispheric (laterality index 0.82), indicating limited right hemisphere language reserve
fMRI findings suggest awake craniotomy with cortical mapping should be strongly considered for maximal safe resection
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates that the left frontal lesion is in close proximity to critical language (Broca area, 0.8 cm) and motor (right hand primary motor cortex, 1.2 cm) regions. Language is strongly left-lateralized. These findings indicate that maximal safe resection will require awake craniotomy with intraoperative cortical stimulation mapping to preserve language and motor function. Complete resection may not be achievable without unacceptable risk of expressive aphasia or right hand weakness.
recommended_actions: Awake craniotomy with cortical stimulation mapping strongly recommended
Preoperative discussion with patient regarding risks of expressive aphasia and right hand weakness
Consider DTI tractography for corticospinal tract and arcuate fasciculus visualization

output.interpretation: Extended blood cultures (aerobic and anaerobic) show no growth at 5 days. Combined with normal procalcitonin and clinical improvement without fever, this significantly reduces the probability of active brain abscess, particularly given that the patient completed a course of oral antibiotics for dental abscess three weeks ago. While a completely walled-off intracranial abscess could theoretically occur with negative blood cultures, the constellation of negative cultures, normal procalcitonin, and advanced imaging characteristics (elevated rCBV on perfusion, elevated choline on spectroscopy, no amino acid peaks) makes brain abscess very unlikely.

Demyelinating Disease Workup.clinical_significance: Rules out neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder
No evidence of optic nerve demyelination

Demyelinating Disease Workup.value: Negative (no bands in CSF or serum)
Demyelinating Disease Workup.clinical_significance: No evidence of intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis; argues against multiple sclerosis

output.interpretation: Comprehensive demyelinating disease workup is negative. NMO-IgG, MOG antibody, visual evoked potentials, CSF oligoclonal bands, and IgG index are all normal. Despite the patient's brother having multiple sclerosis, there is no laboratory evidence to support tumefactive demyelination in this patient. Combined with the perfusion and spectroscopy findings, tumefactive MS can be effectively excluded from the differential diagnosis.

differential.key_distinguishing: Recent dental abscess with possible transient bacteremia provides plausible infectious source. Ring enhancement and central restricted diffusion overlap with abscess features. However, thick irregular walls, corpus callosum involvement, elevated rCBV on perfusion, elevated choline on spectroscopy, and absence of amino acid peaks all argue against abscess. Negative blood cultures and normal procalcitonin further reduce probability.

Family history of MS in brother raised initial suspicion. Ring-enhancing lesions can occur in tumefactive MS. However, typical open-ring enhancement pattern is absent, mass effect is more than expected for demyelination, perfusion shows elevated rCBV (demyelination shows low rCBV), spectroscopy shows tumor pattern, and demyelinating serologies are negative.

Can cross the corpus callosum and present with ring enhancement. However, immunocompetent patients typically show homogeneous enhancement rather than ring enhancement. Low rCBV with restricted diffusion would be more typical of lymphoma. Patient has no immunocompromising conditions.

Ring-enhancing lesion could represent metastasis. However, typically located at the gray-white junction, more spherical, and associated with a known primary cancer. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows no primary malignancy. Corpus callosum crossing and infiltrative white matter extension are atypical for metastasis.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize the patient: initiate anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam 500 mg IV, then 500 mg twice daily), monitor neurological status, and address acute hyperglycemia. Start dexamethasone 10 mg IV loading dose then 4 mg IV every 6 hours for vasogenic edema and mass effect.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Seizure control, reduction of cerebral edema, clinical stabilization

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, procalcitonin), hepatic panel, LDH, HbA1c, and blood cultures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild leukocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers creating diagnostic ambiguity; negative blood cultures; elevated glucose and HbA1c reflecting diabetes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain MRI brain with and without contrast for detailed characterization of the ring-enhancing lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Ring-enhancing lesion with thick irregular walls, satellite lesion, corpus callosum involvement — features atypical for classic abscess but imaging differential remains open

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizure activity and characterize focal dysfunction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Focal left frontotemporal slowing confirming structural pathology; no epileptiform discharges captured on routine recording

optimal_actions.action: Order MR perfusion imaging to differentiate high-grade neoplasm from abscess or demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in enhancing rim (ratio 3.8), strongly favoring high-grade neoplasm over abscess or demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Order MR spectroscopy to further characterize the lesion metabolically
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks without amino acid peaks — classic tumor spectrum, argues strongly against abscess

optimal_actions.action: Discontinue empiric antibiotics once advanced imaging confirms tumor rather than abscess; continue dexamethasone for edema
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Appropriate de-escalation of unnecessary antibiotic therapy

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to exclude a primary extracranial malignancy (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No primary malignancy identified, supporting primary brain tumor diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain neurosurgical consultation for stereotactic biopsy or craniotomy for tissue diagnosis with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q, EGFR, TERT)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4, with EGFR amplification, TERT promoter mutation, unmethylated MGMT

optimal_actions.action: Order fMRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping given proximity to eloquent cortex
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Tumor in close proximity to Broca area and right hand motor cortex; awake craniotomy recommended

optimal_actions.action: Plan maximal safe surgical resection with awake craniotomy and intraoperative cortical stimulation mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Optimal surgical approach balancing extent of resection with preservation of language and motor function

optimal_actions.action: Initiate standard Stupp protocol: concurrent radiation therapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with daily temozolomide 75 mg/m2, followed by adjuvant temozolomide 150-200 mg/m2 for 5 days every 28 days for 6-12 cycles
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard-of-care adjuvant therapy, though benefit of temozolomide may be reduced given unmethylated MGMT promoter

optimal_actions.action: Consider demyelinating disease workup given family history of MS, but do not delay definitive tumor workup
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative demyelinating serologies, excluding tumefactive MS

optimal_actions.action: Consider tumor treating fields (TTFields/Optune) as part of adjuvant therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Additional progression-free and overall survival benefit when combined with temozolomide

critical_actions: Do not assume the ring-enhancing lesion is an abscess solely based on the dental infection history — pursue advanced imaging to differentiate tumor from abscess
Obtain MR perfusion and MR spectroscopy to distinguish high-grade glioma from abscess and other ring-enhancing lesion etiologies
Initiate anti-seizure medication promptly given clinical seizure and structural brain lesion
Start dexamethasone for vasogenic edema and mass effect
Obtain tissue diagnosis via stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection before initiating definitive treatment
Request molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q, EGFR, TERT) on biopsy specimen for accurate WHO 2021 classification and treatment planning
Obtain fMRI for pre-surgical mapping given lesion proximity to eloquent cortex
contraindicated_actions: Treating empirically as brain abscess with prolonged antibiotics without pursuing tissue diagnosis or advanced imaging — delays definitive cancer treatment
Performing emergent surgical drainage assuming abscess without advanced imaging characterization — inappropriate surgical approach for glioblastoma
Dismissing the family history of MS as irrelevant without appropriate workup — tumefactive MS must be considered and excluded
Delaying neurosurgical consultation while waiting for serial imaging to 'see if it responds to antibiotics'
Starting whole-brain radiation without tissue diagnosis and molecular profiling
Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of a mass lesion with midline shift without first addressing mass effect — risk of herniation
Discontinuing anti-seizure medication prematurely in the setting of a structural lesion
key_reasoning_points: The central diagnostic challenge is distinguishing a ring-enhancing lesion in a patient with a recent dental abscess: the infectious history creates a strong cognitive anchor toward brain abscess that must be overcome with systematic advanced imaging
MR perfusion is the single most discriminating imaging modality: elevated rCBV (>1.75) in the enhancing rim strongly favors high-grade neoplasm, while abscess walls typically show low rCBV

MR spectroscopy provides complementary information: amino acid peaks (acetate, succinate) are characteristic of pyogenic abscess, while elevated choline/NAA ratio with lipid/lactate peaks is characteristic of high-grade glioma
The thick, irregular, nodular enhancement pattern and corpus callosum involvement are red flags for glioblastoma — classic abscesses have thinner, smoother walls and rarely cross the corpus callosum

The mild leukocytosis, elevated CRP/ESR, and recent dental infection are red herrings that can lead the clinician astray — these findings are non-specific and can occur with high-grade malignancy or as residual effects of the treated dental infection
A brother with multiple sclerosis adds another layer of diagnostic complexity by introducing tumefactive demyelination into the differential, requiring specific workup to exclude

The largely non-focal neurological examination despite a large frontal lesion is an additional confounding element — more typical of slowly growing or non-destructive lesions, but can occur with frontal lobe gliomas in 'silent' cortical regions

Tissue diagnosis with molecular profiling is essential for accurate WHO 2021 classification and treatment planning — the molecular profile (IDH status, MGMT methylation, EGFR, TERT) directly impacts prognosis and therapeutic decisions

metadata.author: NeuroBench Generator
metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Ring-enhancing lesion with recent dental abscess history mimicking brain abscess; family history of MS adds tumefactive demyelination to the differential. Advanced imaging (perfusion, spectroscopy) required to distinguish GBM from abscess.
